ہرن.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀭𑀺𑀡 (hariṇa), from Sanskrit हरिण (hariṇa, “deer”), from हरि (hari, “reddish brown”).

senses.g: Harnai (a city in Harnai district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ہریانہ.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa हरियाण (hariyāṇa) found in the works of the 1000s CE writer Vibudh Shridhar. Further, probably from a metathesis of Prakrit *𑀅𑀳𑀺𑀭𑀸𑀬𑀡 (*ahirāyaṇa), from 𑀅𑀳𑀺𑀭 (ahira), a variant of Prakrit 𑀆𑀳𑀻𑀭 (āhīra), from Sanskrit आभीर (ābhīra) (the name of a community mentioned in the Mahabharata that inhabited the area of present-day Haryana in ancient times), suffixed with 𑀅𑀬𑀦 ~ 𑀅𑀬𑀡 (ayana ~ ayaṇa), from Sanskrit अयन (ayana, “path, road”).

senses.g: Indus Valley Civilization (an ancient civilization extant along the river Indus in South Asia)

ہزار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هزار (hazār), from Proto-Iranian *hajáhram, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *saȷ́ʰásram, from Proto-Indo-European *sm̥-ǵʰéslom. Doublet of سہس (sahas).

senses.g: of or relating to the Spanish language
Spain (a country in Southern Europe, including most of the Iberian peninsula)

Hispania (the Iberian Peninsula, a peninsula and historical region of Southern Europe covering modern-day Spain, Portugal and Andorra, when under the control of Ancient Rome; split into between two and five Roman provinces, depending on the time period)

ہسپانیہ.e: Borrowed from Portuguese Hispânia, ultimately from Latin Hispania, with the spelling likely influenced by برطانیہ (bartāniyā).

ex.u:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری
صَدِیوں رَہَا ہَے دُشْمَن دَورِ زَماں ہَمارا
kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī
ex.r: sadiyõ rahā hai duśman daur-i zamā̃ hamārā
ex.e:
There is something about our existence for it doesn't get wiped
Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.

ہستی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَسْتِی (hastī). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi ہستی (hsty).

senses.g: week, sennight (a period of seven days)

ex.u: تمہارے انٹرویو کے لئے یہ ضروری ہے کہ میں ایک ہفتہ تمہارے ساتھ رہوں۔
ex.r: qiṣṣe tere, fasāne mere
ex.e: For your interview, it's necessary that I stay with you for a week.

ex.u: بروز ہفتہ شام ساڑھے پانچ بجے اس کی کرنل بخشی سے شادی ہو گئی۔
ex.r: barroz hafta śām sāṛhe pānc baje us kī karnal baxśī se śādī ho ga'ī.
ex.e: On Saturday, half-five in the evening, she married Colonel Bakhshi.

ex.u: اسی سرمائے میں سے قریب کے تھانے میں پولِس کو ہفتہ بھی دیا جاتا ہے۔
ex.r: dīvāron̲ ke bīc
ex.e: With the [same] funds, in the nearby police station, the police are also given bribe[s].

senses.g: Nelson hold (a trick where the opponent's neck and arms are locked).

ex.u: طاقت آزمائی کے بعد دانوں پیچ شروع ہوئے اور اک دستی ، دو دستی، آنٹی سونت ، روم ، ... ہتکوڑہ گال ، گرہ ، جھوم ، ہفتہ ٹنگڑی ، کولا ، سکھی ، جمائل گولا لاٹھی ، .... قفلی ہوتے ہوتے شہزادے نے رخشاں کی کمربند زنجیر میں ہاتھ ڈال ایک ہی قوت میں سر سے بلند کیا ۔
ex.r: Ashraf Ṣabūhī Dahalvī

senses.g: hafta – an old form/genre of poetry

ex.u: ایسے کئی ھندوستانی ، ایرانی اور ترکی شاعر ھیں جنھوں نے پانچ پانچ سات سات ایسے قصے نظم کیے ھیں۔ یہیں سے خمسے اور ھفتے کی بنیاد پڑی
ex.r: gārsā̃ d tāsī
ex.e: There are many such Indian, Iranian and Turkish poets who have composed five or seven such parts. This is where [the forms of] Khamsas and Haftas were founded

ہفتہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَفْتَه (hafta). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi ہَفْتَہ (hfth /⁠hafta⁠/)ِ, By surface analysis, ہَفْت (haft, “seven”) + ہ (h). Doublet of سَپْتَاہ (saptāh). Cognates Cognate with Punjabi ہَفْتَہ (haftah) / ਹਫ਼ਤਾ (haftā), Sindhi هَفْتو (hafto), Nepali हप्ता (haptā), Bengali হপ্তা (hopta).

senses.g: inflection of ہَفْتَہ (hafta, “week; Saturday”): — direct plural
inflection of ہَفْتَہ (hafta, “week; Saturday”): — oblique/vocative singular

ہکا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit हिक्का (hikkā). Doublet of ہِیک (hīk).

ہلاک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَلَاک (halāk), borrowed from Arabic هَلَاك (halāk), from هَلَكَ (halaka), from Proto-Semitic *halak-. First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi ہلاک (hlak /⁠halāk⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هَلَاک (halāk), borrowed from Arabic هَلَاك (halāk), from هَلَكَ (halaka), from Proto-Semitic *halak-. First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi ہلاک (hlak /⁠halāk⁠/).

ہلال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هلال (hilāl), from Arabic هِلَال (hilāl).

ہلانا.e: Urdu verb set ہِلْنا (hilnā) ہِلانا (hilānā) ہِلْوانا (hilvānā) Causative form of ہِلْنا (hilnā) .

ہلو.e: Borrowed from Old Marathi 𑘮𑘯𑘳 (haḷu), from Maharastri Prakrit 𑀳𑀮𑀼𑀅 (halua), which is the metathesis of Sanskrit 𑘩𑘑𑘳 (laghú).

senses.g: first-person plural subjective or objective personal pronoun: we, us; plural of مَیں (ma͠i), مُجھ (mujh)

ex.u: ہَم ہون٘گے کامْیاب۔
ex.r: ham hoṅge kāmyāb.
ex.e: we will be successful.

senses.g: first-person singular subjective or objective personal pronoun: I, me

ہم.e: Inherited from Old Hindi हम (hama), हंम (haṃma), from Apabhramsa अम्हे (amhe), from Prakrit 𑀅𑀫𑁆𑀳𑁂 (amhe), from Sanskrit अस्मे (asme).

senses.g: The speakers/writers, or the speaker/writer and at least one other person; we, us.

ex.u: ہَم پاکِسْتانی بَہُت مِہْمان نَواز ہوتے ہَیں۔
ex.r: ham pākistānī bahut mihmān navāz hote ha͠i.
ex.e: We Pakistanis tend to be very hospitable.

ہم.e: Inherited from Old Hindi हम (hama), हंम (haṃma), from Apabhramsa अम्हे (amhe), from Prakrit 𑀅𑀫𑁆𑀳𑁂 (amhe), from Sanskrit अस्मे (asme).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هَم (ham), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *somHós. Cognate of English same.
Borrowed from Classical Persian هَم (ham), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *somHós. Cognate of English same.

ex.u: کیرالہ کا ہم جنس پرست جوڑا: ’جب ڈاکٹر ہمارے رشتے کو نہیں سمجھ سکے تو دوسرے کیسے سمجھیں گے؟‘
ex.e: Kerala's gay couple: 'If the doctor couldn't understand our relationship, how will others?'

ex.u: Then they came for the homosexuals, and I did not speak up because I was not a homosexual.

senses.g: brother-in-law – sister-in-law's husband (husband of wife's sister)

ہم زلف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَم زُلْف (ham zulf). By surface analysis, ہَم (ham) + زُلْف (zulf).

senses.g: vocative plural of ہَم زُلْف (brother-in-law – sister-in-law's husband (husband of wife's sister))
oblique plural of ہَم زُلْف (brother-in-law – sister-in-law's husband (husband of wife's sister))

ہم عصر.e: Made up with Persian هَم and Arabic عَصَر

senses.g: dative/accusative of ہم (ham); plural of مجھ کو (mujh-ko)

ex.u: ہَم نے کِتاب خَرِیدِی
ex.r: ham ne kitāb xarīdī
ex.e: We bought a book

senses.g: first-person plural possessive pronoun: ours; plural of میرا (merā)

ہمارا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi हमारा (hamārā), हंमारा (haṃmārā), from Apabhramsa अम्हार (amhāra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-) by metathesis; see there for more

senses.g: first-person plural possessive determiner: our; plural of میرا (merā)

ex.u:
سارے جہاں سے اچھا ہندوستاں ہَمارا
ہم بلبلیں ہیں اس کی، یہ گلستاں ہمارا
sāre jahā̃ se acchā hindostā̃ hamārā
ex.r: ham bulbulẽ ha͠i is kī, ye gulistā̃ hamārā
ex.e:
Better than the whole world, is our Hindustan;
We are its nightingales, and it is our flower-garden.

ہمارا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi हमारा (hamārā), हंमारा (haṃmārā), from Apabhramsa अम्हार (amhāra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-) by metathesis; see there for more

senses.g: Himalayas (a mountain range across the entire Indian subcontinent)

ہمالیہ.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Classical Sanskrit हिमालय (himālaya).

ہمایوں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian همایون (humāyūn), from Middle Persian 𐭧𐭥𐭬𐭠𐭩 (Humāy), from Old Persian *Humāyah, from Proto-Iranian *māyā- (“blessing, happiness”) and the prefix *hu- (“good”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁su-.

senses.g: Humayun, the second emperor of the Mughal Empire
a male given name, Humayun, from Persian

ہمایوں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian همایون (humāyūn), from Middle Persian 𐭧𐭥𐭬𐭠𐭩 (Humāy), from Old Persian *Humāyah, from Proto-Iranian *māyā- (“blessing, happiness”) and the prefix *hu- (“good”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁su-.

ہمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هِمَّت (himmat), from Arabic هِمَّة (himma). Compare Gujarati હિંમત (himmat), Marathi हिंमत (himmat), Punjabi ਹਿੰਮਤ (himmat) / ہِمت (himt).

ہمجنسیت.e: From ہم (ham-, “same, homo-”) + جنس (jins, “sex”) + یت (-iyat, “-ism, -ness, -ality”), likely a calque of English homosexuality.

ہمسایہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَمْسَایَه (hamsāya), from Middle Persian [script needed] (hmsʾdk' /⁠hamsāyag⁠/).

ہمسفر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَمْسَفَر (hamsafar); can be constructed as ہَم (ham, “also”) + سَفَر (safar, “travel”).

ex.u: مگر اُس سے ہمنوائی نہ تھی۔
ex.r: magar us se hamnavā'ī na thī.
ex.e: But we weren't compatible.

ہمیشگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian همیشگی (hamēšagī). Equivalent to ہمیشہ (hameśa, “forever”) + ـگی (-gī).

senses.g: contracted dative/accusative of ہم (ham); plural of مجھے (mujhe)

ex.u: تَعْزِیرَاتِ ہِنْد
ex.r: ta'zīrāt-i hind
ex.e: Indian Penal Code

ex.u: پاک ہند سرحد
ex.r: pāk hind sarḥad
ex.e: Indo-Pak border

ہند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هند (hind), from Old Persian 𐏃𐎡𐎯𐎢𐏁 (hiⁿdūš), ultimately from Sanskrit सिन्धु (sindhu, “stream”). Doublet of سندھ (sindh) and انڈیا (inḍiyā).

senses.g: the people of Indian descent who have converted to Islam, found in Peshawar and other parts of Afghanistan.

ہند کی.e: Term is made up with هند + كی, literally means 'of Hind'.

ہندسہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَنْدَسَه (handasa), from Arabic هَنْدَسَة (handasa), itself a Middle Persian borrowing. Doublet of اَنْدَازَہ (andāza).

senses.g: Any subject regarding to Delhi Sultanate, or any other Islamic regime in any part of the Indian subcontinent
Any subject regarding to Mughal Empire

ex.u: ہِنْدوں میں بُو رَہے گِی جَب تَک اِیمان کِی، تَخْتِ لَنْدَن تَک چَلے گِی تیغِ ہِنْدوسْتان کِی
ex.r: hindõ mẽ bū rahe gī jab tak īmān kī, taxt-i landan tak cale gī teġ-i hindostān kī
ex.e: As long as Hindoos have even an ounce of honesty and dignity-So long shall the sword of Hindustan flash before the throne of London.

ہندوتوا.e: From Hindi हिंदुत्व (hindutva), from Bengali হিন্দুত্ব (hindutto).

ex.u: ہِنْدوں میں بُو رَہے گِی جَب تَک اِیمان کِی، تَخْتِ لَنْدَن تَک چَلے گِی تیغِ ہِنْدوسْتان کِی
ex.r: hindõ mẽ bū rahe gī jab tak īmān kī, taxt-i landan tak cale gī teġ-i hindostān kī
ex.e: As long as Hindoos have even an ounce of honesty and dignity-So long shall the sword of Hindustan flash before the throne of London.

ہندوستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هندوستان (hindustān). Equivalent to ہندو (hindū, “Indian”) + ـستان (-stān, “-land”).

senses.g: Indian (of, from or relating to India)
Hindu Kush (a mountain range in Afghanistan and Pakistan)
of or relating to Indian people, or semantically their land and culture

ہندوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هندوی (hindavī); equivalent to ہندو (hindū, “Indian”) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هندوی (hindavī); equivalent to ہندو (hindū, “Indian”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: belonging or relating to India, anything Indian
native, inherited in the Urdu language, as opposed to a borrowing.

ہندی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هِنْدِی (hindī), from Arabic هِنْدِي (hindī).

senses.g: Modern Standard Hindi: a khariboli based tongue which happens to be standardised and Sanskritised version of the Hindustani language.
All the lects in the Hindi Belt, which also includes lects that do not belong to the Central Zone of Indo-Aryan languages.
the language now known as Urdu.

ex.u: اور الہامات مولف براہین سے (انگریزی میں ہوں خوا ہندی و عربی وغیرہ) آج تک ایک بھی جھوٹ نہیں نکلا
ex.r: rīviyū barāhīn-i ahmadiyya
ex.e: And the revelations of the complier of these proofs (whether they be in English, or in Hindi, or Arabic etc.), to this day have never been proven false

ہندی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هِنْدِی (hindī), from Arabic هِنْدِي (hindī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هِنْدِی (hindī), from Arabic هِنْدِي (hindī).

senses.g: official name of the Mughal Empire in Arabic.

ہنڈار.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *हुण्डहार (*huṇḍahāra). Cognate with Nepali हुँडार (hũḍār), Odia ହୁନାଁର୍ (hunā̃r).

ہنڈی.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हुण्डिका (huṇḍikā). Cognate with Punjabi ਹੁੰਡੀ (huṇḍī) / ہُنْڈِی (huṇḍī), Sindhi 𑋞𑋣𑋟𑋈𑋢 (huṇḍī) / हुंडी (huṇḍī) / هُنڊِي (huṇḍī), Assamese হুণ্ডী (hundi), Bengali হুন্ডি (hunḍi), Odia ହୁଣ୍ଡି (huṇḍi), Gujarati હૂંડી (hū̃ḍī), Marathi हुंडी (huṇḍī).

senses.g: Hounslow (a town in western Greater London, England)

ہنسنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀲𑀤𑀺 (hasadi), from Sanskrit हसति (hásati). Cognate with Punjabi ਹੱਸਣਾ (hassaṇā), Nepali हाँस्नु (hā̃snu).

ہنہنانا.e: Possibly onomatopoeic. Cognate with Gujarati હણહણવું (haṇhaṇvũ), Punjabi ਹਿਣਹਿਣਾਉਣਾ (hiṇhiṇāuṇā).

senses.g: inflection of ہونا (honā, “to be”): — second-person plural present indicative
inflection of ہونا (honā, “to be”): — second/third-person singular present subjunctive
inflection of ہونا (honā, “to be”): — second-person plural present subjunctive
inflection of ہونا (honā, “to be”): — second-person singular present imperative
inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine singular perfective participle
inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine singular perfect indicative

ہوا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑀽𑀤 (bhūda), from Sanskrit भूत (bhūta, “become, been”), perfect participle of भवति (bhavati, “to become”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هَوَا (hawā), from Arabic هَوَاء (hawāʔ).

ہوائی.e: Borrowed from Arabic هَوَائِيّ (hawāʔiyy). By surface analysis, ہَوا (havā) + ـی (-ī).

ex.u: مَیں دیر سے ہَوائی اَڈّے پَر پَہُن٘چا اَور جَہاز میرے بَغَیر اُڑ گَیا۔
ex.r: ma͠i der se havāī aḍḍe par pahũcā aur jahāz mere baġair uṛ gayā.
ex.e: I arrived at the airport late and the plane took off without me.

ہوائی جہاز.e: From ہَوائی (havāī, “relating to air, aero-”) + جَہاز (jahāz, “craft”). Calque of English aircraft.

senses.g: inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine singular habitual participle
inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine singular contrafactual
a male given name from Arabic

ہود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هود (hūd), from Arabic هُود (hūd).

ہوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَوَس (hawas), from Arabic هَوَس (hawas). Cognate with Gujarati હવસ (havas), Marathi हौस (haus), Punjabi ਹਵਸ (havas) / ہَوَس (hauas).

ہوش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هوش (hōš). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi ہوش (hvś /⁠hoś⁠/).

senses.g: Hoshangabad (a city in Madhya Pradesh, India)

ہوشنگ آباد.e: Named after Hoshang Shah, from ہوشَن٘گ (hośaṅg) + ـآباد (-ābād).

senses.g: Any ritual in which making offerings into a consecrated fire is the primary action.

ہونا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑁄𑀤𑀺 (bhodi), from Sanskrit भवति (bhávati, “to happen, to become”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰáwHati, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéwHeti (“to grow, become, come into being, appear”).

ہونٹ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀑𑀝𑁆𑀞 (oṭṭha), 𑀳𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀞 (hoṭṭha), from Sanskrit ओष्ठ (óṣṭha). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi ہونٿ (hvnṭ).

senses.g: first-person singular present indicative/subjunctive of ہونا (honā)
inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine plural perfective participle
inflection of ہونا (honā): — masculine plural perfect indicative

ہوئے.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

ex.u: مَیں ہی یہ کام خَتَم کَرُوں گا۔
ex.r: ma͠i hī ye kām xatam karū̃ gā.
ex.e: Only I will finish this work.

ہی.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हि (hi, “indeed, surely, of course, certainly”, emphatic particle).

ex.u: بازار میں ہیجڑے بھیکھ مانگ رہے تھے۔
ex.r: bāzār mẽ hījṛe bhīkh māṅg rahe the.
ex.e: At the shop, hijras were begging.

senses.g: a homosexual male; fag, faggot — a cowardly male

ہیر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀻𑀭 (hīra), from Sanskrit हीर (hīra).

senses.g: Heer, the female tragic lover from a classical Punjabi romantic legend

ہیر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀻𑀭 (hīra), from Sanskrit हीर (hīra).

senses.g: a necklace made from precious stones

ہیرا.e: Inherited from Prakrit हीरग (hīraga), from Sanskrit हीरक (hīraka).
Inherited from Prakrit हीरग (hīraga), from Sanskrit हीरक (hīraka).

ہیک.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हिक्का (hikkā). Doublet of ہِکَّا (hikkā), a tatsama.

senses.g: Hamburg (a state of Germany containing the city of the same name)

ہیمبرگ.e: Borrowed from English Hamburg, or directly from German Hamburg.

ہینسنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀳𑀺𑀁𑀲𑀇 (*hiṃsaï) (compare 𑀳𑀺𑀁𑀲𑀸 (hiṃsā, “neighing”)), from Sanskrit हेषति (heṣati).

senses.g: first/third-person plural present indicative of ہونا (honā): (we, they) are
third-person singular present indicative of ہونا (honā, “to be”)

ex.u: وہ پاگَل ہَے۔
ex.r: vo pāgal hai.
ex.e: He is mad.

ہے.e: There are similar words in Old Hindi to mean "is", आहि (āhi), है (hai)—the source of this word—, and होइ (hoi)—the source of the modern subjunctive ہو (ho), among others. होइ (hoi) is clearly from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑁄𑀤𑀺 (bhodi). The first two are possibly from Apabhramsa *अहहि (ahahi) or *अहै (*ahai), a thematic variant of the Prakrit root 𑀅𑀲𑁆 (as), from अस् (as), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁es-, making it a cognate to English is and Spanish es. Though the s > h shift and the thematisation of Old Indo-Aryan roots was common in Middle Indo-Aryan, neither are noted by Prakrit grammarians for अस् (as), making this derivation dubious. They may otherwise be connected with Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑁄𑀤𑀺 (bhodi) and Sanskrit भवति (bhavati), or at least influenced by this root. Cognate with Magahi 𑂯𑂶 (hai), Marathi आहे (āhe), Bengali আছে (ache), Maithili अछि (achi), Gujarati છે (che).

ex.u: ہے خُدایَہ
ex.r: he xudāya
ex.e: Oh my God

ہے.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀳𑁂 (he), from Sanskrit हे (he).

senses.g: The thirty-fifth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ہ and followed by ء; it has no sound value on its own, but causes the preceding consonant to be aspirated.
The thirty-fifth letter of the Urdu alphabet, called ہَمْزَہ (hamza, “Hamza”) and written in the Arabic script.
The thirty-eighth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ء and followed by ے.
relating to, forms adjectives from nouns

ex.u: جوکِھم (jokhim, “risk”) + ـِی (-ī) → جوکِھمِی (jokhimī, “risky”)

ـی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit -𑀇𑀅 (-ia), from Sanskrit -इक (-ika, diminutive suffix) or Sanskrit -ईय (-īya, adjectival suffix). Later influenced by Persian ـی (-i).

ex.u: ساتھ (sāth, “company, support”) + ـی (-ī) → ساتھی (sāthī, “companion, partner”)

ـی.e: Borrowed from the nominative singular form of Sanskrit -इन् (-in, doer, possessor).

senses.g: forms abstract nouns from adjectives or common nouns

ex.u: روشن (rauśan, “bright”) + ـی (-ī) → روشنی (rauśnī, “brightness”)

ـی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ـِی (-ī), from Middle Persian 𐭩𐭧 (yḥ /⁠-īh⁠/).

senses.g: denoting the science, study or branch of knowledge of the thing denoted by the word it is suffixed to: -logy, -ology

یات.e: Borrowed from Arabic ـِيَّات (-iyyāt). Compare Hindi -विद्या (-vidyā) or Hindi -इकी (-ikī).

یاتری.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit यात्रिन् (yātrin). Related to یاترا (yātrā, “journey; pilgrimage”).

یاس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَاس (yās), a variant of یَأْس (ya's), in turn derived from Arabic يَأْس (yaʔs).

senses.g: a female given name, Yasameen or Yasmeen, from Persian, equivalent to English Jasmine

یاقوت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یاقوت (yāqūt), from Arabic يَاقُوت (yāqūt), ultimately from Ancient Greek ὑάκινθος (huákinthos).

senses.g: mane (of a horse; — orig. 'neck of a horse')
denoting a state of being the thing denoted by the word it is suffixed to: -ness, -ity, -dom

ex.u: قَوم (qaum, “nation”) + ـِیَت (-iyat) → قَومِیَت (qaumiyat, “nationality”)

یت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ـِیَّت (-iyyat), in turn from Arabic ـِيَّة (-iyya). Compare Hindi -ता (-tā) and Hindi -त्व (-tva).

senses.g: a particular measure of length (equal to 4 hastas)

یتیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یتیم (yatīm), from Arabic يَتِيم (yatīm).
Borrowed from Classical Persian یتیم (yatīm), from Arabic يَتِيم (yatīm).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

یحیی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یحیی (yahyā), from Arabic يَحْيَى (yaḥyā).

یخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَخ (yax), in turn Derived from Proto-Iranian *Háyxam, ultimately derived from Proto-Indo-European *h₁eyH-. Cognate of English ice.

senses.g: yakhni; a stew of meat or fish

یرغمال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یرغمال (yarġamāl), from Chagatai یرغمال (yarğamāl).

senses.g: Jeremiah, a prophet; author of the Book of Jeremiah and the Lamentations
Jerusalem (a city in Israel; a city in Palestine)

ex.u: اسرائیلی اور فلسطینی دونوں یروشلم پر اپنا دعویٰ رکھتے ہیں اور اس کا تنازع بہت پرانا ہے۔
ex.r: isrā'īlī aur falast̤īnī donoṉ yarūśalam par apnā daʻvá rakhte haiṉ aur is kā tanāzaʻ bahut purānā hai.
ex.e: Israel and Palestine both claim Jerusalem and the dispute is quite old.

یروشلم.e: Borrowed from Aramaic יְרוּשְׁלֶם (yərūšəlem), possibly a learned borrowing. Compare Hebrew יְרוּשָׁלֵם (y'rūšálem).

senses.g: God, the creator of good, Goddess of righteousness

ex.u:
فکر یزیدیت کے لیے سدِّ راہ ہے
اس کے ہی دم سے اشھد اَن لا الہ ہے
ex.r: sed kfāet nqvī
ex.e: Vajdān Publications

senses.g: the Death of Imam Hussain (or generally the Battle of Karbala)

یزیدیت.e: From یزید (yazīd) + ـیت (-iyat). Named after یزید بن معاویہ (Yazīd ibn Muʿāwiya), the killer of Imam Hussain at Karbala.

senses.g: the Book of Isaiah (one of the books of the Bible)
Isaiah (a prophet, the author of the Book of Isaiah)

یسوع.e: Borrowed from Arabic يَسُوع (yasūʕ), from Aramaic ישוע (yešú‘), from Hebrew ישוע (yešûa‘), a contracted form of יהושע (yəhôšua‘, “Joshua”).

senses.g: Jesuit, a member of the Society of Jesus
a male given name from Arabic يَعْقُوب (yaʕqūb) [in turn from Classical Syriac ܝܥܩܘܒ, in turn from Hebrew יעקב], equivalent to English Jacob

یعقوب.e: Borrowed from Arabic يَعْقُوب (yaʕqūb), from Classical Syriac ܝܥܩܘܒ (yaʿqōḇ), from Biblical Hebrew יעקב.

یعنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَعْنِی (ya'nī), from Arabic يَعْنِي (yaʕnī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian یَعْنِی (ya'nī), from Arabic يَعْنِي (yaʕnī).

یقین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَقِین (yaqīn), from Arabic يَقِين (yaqīn).
Borrowed from Classical Persian یَقِین (yaqīn), from Arabic يَقِين (yaqīn).

senses.g: indeed, definitely, for sure, surely, undoubtedly

یقینا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَقِیناً (yaqīnan), from Arabic يَقِينًا (yaqīnan).

یک مشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یک مشت (yak mušt), By surface analysis, یک (yak, “one”) + مشت (muśt, “fist; handful”).

senses.g: one, single, sole; singular, unique, unrivalled, incomparable
of one opinion, agreed, unanimous; united
incursion, inroad, attack, assault, forced march (of an army)

ex.u:
ایک کَہانِی ہَے جو سَب کو سُنانِی ہَے
اِن کِی بُھوک بِھی تو مَیں نے ہِی مِٹانِی ہَے
یَلْغار ہو! یَلْغار ہو!
یَلْغار ہو! یَلْغار ہو!
ex.r:
ek kahānī hai jo sab ko sunānī hai
in kī bhūk bhī to ma͠i ne hī miṭānī hai
yalġār ho! yalġār ho!
yalġār ho! yalġār ho!
ex.e:
There is a story that I want to tell everyone
I need to satisfy their hunger after all
Let’s attack! Let’s attack!
Let’s attack! Let’s attack!

یلغار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِیلْغَار (īlġār), of Turkic origin. First attested in c. 1667 as Middle Hindi يلغار (ylġar).

senses.g: lord Yama, god of the underworld
Yemen (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

یمن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یمن (yaman), from Arabic اَلْيَمَن (al-yaman).

senses.g: Yamuna, Jumna (a river in the states of Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh and Haryana and union territory of Delhi, India)
Suffix which form the plural of nouns that do not end with ی (ye).
Any of the New Testament figures named John

ex.u: یوحنا اصطباغی
ex.e: John the Baptist (see also یَحییٰ (yahyā))

senses.g: a male given name, equivalent to English John

یوحنا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یوحنا (yūhannā), from Arabic يُوحَنَّا (yūḥannā), from Aramaic יוחנן (yoḥanan).

یوحنا اصطباغی.e: Calque of English John the Baptist.

senses.g: Europe (a continent located west of Asia and north of Africa)
ellipsis of یورپی اتحاد (yūrapī ittihād, “European Union”)

یورپ.e: Borrowed from English Europe, ultimately from Ancient Greek Εὐρώπη (Eurṓpē).

یورش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یورش (yūriš), from Chagatai یورش (yöriš).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

یوسف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یُوسُف (yūsuf), borrowed from Arabic يُوسُف (yūsuf), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew יוֹסֵף (yōsép), from הוֹסִיף (hosíf, “to add”).

ex.u:
لب شیریں ہے مصری یوسف ثانی ہے یہ لڑکا
نہ چھوڑے گا میرا دل چاہ کنعانی ہے یہ لڑکا
lab-e-śīrī̃ hai misrī yūsuf-e-sānī hai ye laṛkā
ex.r: na choṛegā merā dil cāh-e-kan'ānī hai ye laṛkā
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

یوسف ثانی.e: Ezafe compound of یوسف (yūsuf, “Joseph”) + ثانی (sānī, “second”), literally “the second Joseph”.

یوغ.e: PIE word *yugóm Borrowed from Classical Persian یوغ (yōġ, “yoke”). Cognate with Pashto جغ (źëǧ, “yoke”), Torwali یو (yū, “yoke”), and Kalasha جو (ǰu, “yoke”).

senses.g: Ukraine (a country in Europe; capital: Kyiv)

ex.u: عَلِی گَڑھْ مُسْلِم یُونِیوَرْسِٹی نے مُسَلْمانوں کی تَعْلِیمی تَرَقِّی کے لِئے بَہُت یوگْدان دِیا ہَیں.
ex.r: 'alī gaṛh muslim yūnīvarsiṭī ne musalmānõ kī ta'līmī taraqqī ke lie bahut yogdān diyā ha͠i.
ex.e: Aligarh Muslim University have contributed greatly for the educational development of Muslims.

senses.g: Uganda (a country in the East Africa, Africa)
fit, fitting, becoming suitable, suited, proper, appropriate, adequate, competent, capable.

ex.u: یومِ آزادی
ex.r: yaum-e āzādī
ex.e: Independence Day

senses.g: aeon; epoch; period; age; era; (usually in the plural)

یوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَوْم (yawm), from Arabic يَوْم (yawm).

ex.u: ۱۴ اگست پاکستان کا یومِ آزادی ہے
ex.r: caudā agast pākistān kā yaum-e-āzādī hai
ex.e: 14th August is Pakistan's Independence Day

یوم تعطیل.e: Compound of Classical Persian یوم (yawm) + تعطیل (ta'tīl), the first element from Arabic يَوْم (yawm, “day”), the second from Arabic verbal noun تَعْطِيل (taʕṭīl, “suspension, break, hindrance”), from verb عَطَّلَ (ʕaṭṭala, “to suspend, to inactivate”).

یوم مزدور.e: Ezafe compound of یوم (yaum, “day”) + مزدور (mazdūr, “laborer, worker”)

senses.g: a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — a Greek, an Ionian
a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — a Bactrian
a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — a Parthian, a Pahlavi
a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — a Scythian
a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — an Arab, a Turk, a Muslim
a foreigner, an outsider not part of the four-tiers of varna — a European, a Westerner
a follower of a religion of foreign (usually of non-Dharmic origin)

یون.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit यवन (yavana), itself from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀬𑁄𑀦 (yona), from Old Persian 𐎹𐎢𐎴 (y-u-n /⁠yauna⁠/). Doublet of یونان (yūnān) and جون (javan).

یونان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یونان (yunān), ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἰωνία (Iōnía, “Ionia”).

senses.g: Unani (Muslim traditional medicine based on the Hellenistic concept of four humours)
United Kingdom (a kingdom and country in Northern Europe)

یونس.e: Borrowed from Arabic يُونُس (yūnus), from Ancient Greek Ἰωνᾶς (Iōnâs), from Hebrew יונה (yônā).

senses.g: union territory (an administrative division of India that is ruled directly by the federal government, as opposed to a state or province)
this; he; she; it; they (proximal third person personal pronoun)

یہ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi यहु (yahu), from Apabhramsa एहु m (ehu), एह f (eha), from Prakrit 𑀏𑀲𑁄 m (eso), 𑀏𑀲𑀸 f (esā), from Sanskrit एषः m (eṣaḥ), एषा f (eṣā).
Inherited from Old Hindi यहु (yahu), from Apabhramsa एहु m (ehu), एह f (eha), from Prakrit 𑀏𑀲𑁄 m (eso), 𑀏𑀲𑀸 f (esā), from Sanskrit एषः m (eṣaḥ), एषा f (eṣā).

یہود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یهود (yahūd), from Arabic يَهُود (yahūd)

یہودنی.e: From یہود (yahūd) + Sanskrit -इनी (-inī, Sanskrit feminine doer suffix) / + ـنی (-nī, “-ess”).

یہودی.e: Borrowed from Arabic يَهُودِيّ (yahūdiyy), from Hebrew יהודי (yehudí). Cognate with Assamese ইহুদী (ihudi), Bengali ইহুদি (ihudi), Nepali यंहुदि (yaṅhudi), Gujarati યહૂદી (yahūdī), Punjabi یَہُودِی (yahūdī).

Borrowed from Arabic يَهُودِيّ (yahūdiyy), from Hebrew יהודי (yehudí). Cognate with Assamese ইহুদী (ihudi), Bengali ইহুদি (ihudi), Nepali यंहुदि (yaṅhudi), Gujarati યહૂદી (yahūdī), Punjabi یَہُودِی (yahūdī).

یہودیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian یَهُودِیَت (yahūdiyat), from Arabic يَهُودِيَّة (yahūdiyya). Equivalent to یَہُود (yahūd) + ـِیَت (-iyat).

senses.g: Judith - Wise Woman of Bethulia and the protagonist of the Biblical book of Judith

یہودیت.e: Borrowed from Hebrew יְהוּדִית (y'hudít, “woman from Judea, Jewess”).

senses.g: Judea (an ancient region in ancient Israel)

یہودیہ.e: Borrowed from Latin Iūdaea, from Ancient Greek Ἰουδαία (Ioudaía), from Biblical Hebrew יְהוּדָה (yehudá).

senses.g: The thirty-ninth and last letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ی; used at the end of a word to indicate the vowel sounds e and ai.

ex.u: نئؠ
ex.r: na'ye
ex.e: new
